title: Understanding UK Livestock Movement Recording
subtitle: Online reporting systems, the six-day standstill, and what happens if you get it wrong
excerpt: Moving livestock in the UK triggers legal reporting requirements. Different species use different online systems. Getting the process right keeps you compliant and helps control disease spread.
sourceNotes: DEFRA guidance on livestock movement reporting; BCMS (British Cattle Movement Service) registration guidance; APHA eAML2 pig movement system documentation; ARAMS (Animal Reporting and Movement Service) sheep and goat guidance. Synthesised from official UK government sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: Electronic Animal Movement Licence 2. The online system used to record pig movements in England and Wales.
Animal Reporting and Movement Service. The online system used to record sheep and goat movements in England.
British Cattle Movement Service. The government body that maintains the Cattle Tracing System (CTS) and issues cattle passports.
A legal restriction that prevents most livestock from moving off a holding for six days after other livestock have arrived. Designed to limit disease spread.
A document required for moving cattle or pigs, recording the origin, destination, and identification of the animals being moved.

content.text: Why movement recording exists

The UK government tracks livestock movements to control disease. If foot-and-mouth or another serious outbreak occurs, tracing where animals have been is critical. Every keeper plays a part in that system. Reporting is not optional and is not just for large commercial farms.
Cattle: BCMS and the Cattle Tracing System
Cattle movements are recorded through the
BCMS

Cattle Tracing System (CTS). Every cattle movement must be reported within three days. You can report online at the CTS Online portal. Alternatively, you can use paper forms sent by post, but online is faster and recommended.
Each bovine must have a cattle passport. When an animal is born, you register it with BCMS within 27 days. The passport follows the animal for its entire life. You need the
animal movement licence
when cattle move off your holding.
Pigs: eAML2
Pig movements in England and Wales are recorded through
eAML2
. You must create a movement record before pigs leave your holding. The receiving keeper must confirm receipt within three days of arrival. Both parties are responsible for accurate reporting.

Register for eAML2 at the APHA website before you move any pigs. Your holding number links to your account. Pigs moving to slaughter also require an eAML2 record and a Food Chain Information (FCI) form.
Sheep and goats: ARAMS
Sheep and goat movements in England use
ARAMS
. This replaced the old paper-based Annual Inventory and movement document system. ARAMS is web-based and requires registration with APHA. You report each movement within three days.
Sheep and goats also require an annual inventory submission in December. This records how many animals are on your holding at year end. ARAMS handles this alongside movement records.
The six-day standstill
The
six-day standstill

applies when you bring animals onto your holding. After pigs, sheep, goats, or cattle arrive, you cannot move susceptible animals off your holding for six days. The standstill clock starts on the day of arrival.

There are exemptions. Animals moved directly to slaughter are usually exempt. Animals that have lived on your holding continuously for more than six months before a new arrival may also be exempt in some circumstances. Check current APHA guidance, as exemption rules are detailed and change periodically.
Plan your purchase and sale dates around the standstill. Buying at a market then planning to sell at another market three days later will likely breach the rule.
Penalties for non-compliance
Failure to report movements is a criminal offence. APHA inspectors can visit your holding without prior notice. Penalties include:
Fixed penalty notices
Prosecution in a magistrates court
Cross-compliance penalties that affect any farm subsidies you receive
Movement restrictions placed on your holding

Most enforcement actions start with a warning on first offence. Repeat failures or deliberate avoidance are treated more seriously. The reputational and practical impact of a movement restriction far outweighs the time it takes to file reports correctly.
Getting set up before your first animal arrives
Register your holding and get your CPH number from the Rural Payments Agency
Register for the relevant online system for your species (BCMS, eAML2, or ARAMS)
Complete a test login and familiarise yourself with the interface before you need it urgently
Save the APHA helpline number in your phone
